title: Stage 2 — The Advancing Phase
subtitle: The only stage where the method says: buy

lessons.title: The Breakout Signal
lessons.visual_caption: Dixon Technologies — See how Stage 2 begins with a clean break above the Stage 1 resistance level. The 30-week SMA curves upward. This is the entry signal.
lessons.content:
Stage 2 is the only phase where Weinstein says to buy. Everything else in the method exists to get you into Stage 2 stocks at the right time.

Trend Direction: A new uptrend begins — higher highs and higher lows.

Price Action: The breakout occurs when the weekly close moves clearly above the Stage 1 resistance ceiling. Not a brief spike above it — a decisive close above it.

The Master Line: The 30-week SMA turns up and slopes clearly upward. Price must hold above this rising line. A rising 30-week SMA is non-negotiable for a valid Stage 2.

Volume Confirmation — The decisive rule: breakout-week volume must be at least twice (2x) the recent weekly average. Without it, treat the breakout as fake.

Look at the Dixon chart: after the long Stage 1 base, the breakout above resistance came with a surge in volume and the 30-week SMA began curving upward. That combination is the Stage 2 entry signal.

→ Breakout = weekly close clearly above resistance
→ 30-week SMA must be rising, not flat
→ Volume must be at least 2x the average that week
→ All three must be true for a confirmed Stage 2

lessons.title: The Two Buying Zones
lessons.visual_caption: Two safe entry zones: the initial breakout, and the pullback kiss to the rising 30-week SMA.
lessons.content:
There are exactly two safe places to buy a Stage 2 stock. Never buy outside them.

Entry 1 — The Breakout:
Buy a portion the week price closes above resistance on heavy volume. This is the primary signal. You will not always catch it perfectly — that is acceptable. Getting in during the first few weeks of a Stage 2 advance is still well within the safe zone.

Entry 2 — The Pullback Kiss:
After the breakout, price often dips back on low volume to touch the rising 30-week line. This is called a pullback kiss. It is actually the better entry in many cases:
→ Lower price than the breakout
→ Lower risk since the 30-week SMA is right below as support
→ Low volume on the pullback confirms it is just a rest, not a reversal

Buy as price bounces off that line on the pullback.

How to tell a healthy pullback from a dangerous one:
Healthy: Price pulls back on declining volume, the 30-week SMA is still rising, price stays above the SMA
Dangerous: Volume picks up on the pullback, price closes below the 30-week SMA

→ Entry 1: The breakout week on 2x volume
→ Entry 2: The low-volume pullback kiss to the rising SMA
→ Both entries are valid — choose based on what you see
→ Never chase a stock far extended above its SMA

lessons.title: The Overextended (+10%) Rule
lessons.visual_caption: The entry zone: 0-10% above the rising 30-week SMA. Beyond 10% = overextended. Wait for the pullback.
lessons.content:
This is the single rule late buyers most often break — and it causes the most pain.

The Rule: If price is more than 10% above the 30-week SMA, the stock is overextended. Do not buy here — the risk is high and the reward is low.

Why does this matter? A stock stretched far above its average will eventually snap back to it. This is mathematical reality — the average pulls price back like a rubber band. Anyone who bought at 15-20% above the SMA will face a painful pullback even if the stock eventually continues higher.

What to do instead: Wait for a pullback kiss back toward the line. In a healthy Stage 2 stock, these pullbacks come regularly. Patience here is not weakness — it is discipline.

The Safe Entry Zone:
→ 0-10% above the rising 30-week SMA = ideal entry zone
→ The SMA support is close enough to provide a clear stop reference
→ Risk is defined and manageable

Beyond 10%:
→ Do not buy
→ Set a price alert for when it pulls back to the SMA zone
→ Wait — the next opportunity will come

Note: Short-term traders use a faster entry on the daily chart. All short-term timing rules are covered in Module 8.

→ Beyond 10% above SMA = overextended, skip it
→ Wait for the pullback kiss to the SMA
→ Patience at this stage protects your capital

questions.question: What must happen to volume during a valid Stage 2 breakout week?
options: It must drop to its lowest historical level
It must stay exactly at the average
It must rise to at least two times the recent average
Volume does not matter on a breakout
questions.explanation: High volume proves institutions are buying aggressively. A breakout on low volume is highly likely to fail — there is no institutional conviction behind it.

questions.question: Where are the two safe buying zones in a Stage 2 stock?
options: At the breakout on high volume, and on a low-volume pullback to the rising average
In a sideways trend, and when price drops below the average
When price is 20% above the line, and when volume is quiet
Only the day before an earnings report
questions.explanation: Weinstein allows buying at the initial high-volume breakout or during a low-volume pullback that kisses the rising 30-week line. Both entries keep risk manageable.

questions.question: Why is it dangerous to buy a stock trading 15% above its rising 30-week line?
options: Because the stock is already in Stage 4
Because it is overextended — high risk and low reward
Because the average line stops moving
Because volume always vanishes when price rises
questions.explanation: A price stretched beyond 10% above its average is overextended and tends to snap back to the line, handing losses to late buyers. The rubber band always pulls back.

questions.question: What is a pullback kiss entry?
options: Price drops below support on heavy volume
Price dips on low volume to touch the rising 30-week SMA, then bounces
Price moves sideways at the top of Stage 3
questions.explanation: A pullback kiss is often the best entry: a strong Stage 2 stock briefly touches its rising 30-week line on light volume and resumes upward. Risk is low because the SMA is right below as support.
